# 21 POPULUXE Thomas Hine (1987)

## TAKING OFF

Populuxe is a synthetic word, created in the spiri of the many coined words of the time. Madison Avenue kept inventing words like 'autodynamic,' which described a shape of car which made no sense aerodynamically. [..] Like these synthetic words, Populuxe has readily identihable roots, and it reaches toward an ineffable emotion. It derives, of course, from populism and populariy, with just a Aceting allusion to pop art, which took Populuxe imagery and attitudes as subject matter. And it has luxury, popular luxury, luxury for all. This may bc a contradiction in terms, buc it is an expression of the spirit of the time and the rationale for many of the products that were produced. And, fnally, Populuxe contains a thoroughly unnecessary 'e,' to give it class. That final embellishment of a practical and straightforward invention is what makes the word Populuxe, well, Populuxe. [..]

The decade's description of the average American probably fit very few of its citizens. It excluded not only blacks but residents of ethnic urban neighborhoods, the single, widowed and divorced and cheir children and a great many others who did not often show their faces in advertisements. Yet what was remarkable was not that many were excluded from the bounty of a prosperous time, but that a vast majority got a share of the wealth and were able to take large steps up the economic ladder. [...] In this time of great change people chose most often to celebrate change itself. 'We are moving ahead, John Kennedy said repearedly during his 1960 presidential campaign. 'But we are not moving ahead rapidly enough.' Forward motion at ever-increasing speed was what Americans expected from their nation, their cars and their careers. [...]

Poulue arose wel afer Word War I despice the orgy of consumption that characterized the immediate postwar years. The initial postwar period, from about 1946 to 1954, produced a lot of cars, a lot of babies, a lot of appliances, a lor of suburbs. Americans were catching up on the consumption they had put off during World War 11 and the Depression. [...] The turning point came in 1954, an eventful year by any standard. It brought not only the downfall of McCarthy and the momentous Supremc Court decision outlawing scgregated schools but also the introduction of sleck, powerful and finny low-priced cars and the emergence of a sexy, urgent new kind of popular music  rock and roll. Some 1.5 million new homes werc built that year, the great majority of them outside the central cities; 1.4 million power lawn mowers were sold and 4 million babies were born. It was a year in which Americans began to feel less threatened by Communism, and more anxious to enjoy the fruits of American afluence. And it was also a year in which major corporations changed ther marketing strategis in orer t p  . [...]

Populuxe is vulgar by definition. It is the result of an unprecedented ability to acquire, reaching well down into the working class, to the sort of people who had historically been able to have only a few mean objects. These people did not acquire the good simple objects many tastemakers advocated. They had had it with simple, and now they wanted more. [...]

Aulu ec lr appeal, and could be found nearly everywhere, their overwrought design was primarily reserved for private indulgence, not public gestures. Dur-European modernism, interpreted in a strict, rather ascetic way. While much of what Americans were buying was termed 'modernistic, it was hardly modern in the way that tastemakers used that term. This led to a reversal of the age-old pattern in which seats of government and houses of worship received the highest craftsmanship and ornamentation a society could produce, while the individual house was relatively bare. During che Populuxe era, the town hall and even the church or synagogue might be a fearureless glass-andsteel crate, while the individual home would be carefully landscaped and lavishly furnished with things that evoked different historical periods and the future besides. A few architects, notably Eero Saarinen and Edward Durrell Stone, caught the public imagination and contributed to the development of Populuxe design. But for most design professionals, the Populuxe attitude bordered on the immoral. The Museum of Modern Arr, for example, was an aggressive propagandist for European modernism and simplicity, and even some of the home and design magazines and mass magazines such as Life offered forums for those who warned Americans against ornamental overindulgence. They listened politely, then, like adolescents who have come into a windfall, went on a binge.

## THE LUCKIEST GENERATION

America had been able to turn its war machine inco a consumer economy almost overnight.

Productivity rose at a rate of better than 2 percent a year for most of the decade after World War II. American industry was using half the world's steel and oil. American consumers were able to buy three-quarters of the cars and appliances on carth. Real income, after very modest inflation, was on the rise. Indeed, by 1953, the average income per person was half again thc figure in 1929, and although the special conditions of the war made comparisons difficulc, it is clear that ncarly all this growth occurred after 1945. [..]

Better-paid jobs running or maintaining the machinery of automation increased much faster than the low-paid jobs were declining. People in factories, the very heart of the working class, were rapidly ascending to middle-class incomes. The average industrial wage rare had doubled from pre-Depression levels, and there were a lot more jobs available. (...) The postwar period brought a much more equitable distribution of income than ever before. The increase in real income went almost entirely to the middle class. The absolure number of high-income people, which Fortune defined as those making more than \$7,500 annually in 1953 dollars, more than doubled from 1929, but their share of the nation's total income declined sharply. The biggest increase came in the number of families in the \$4,000-\$7,000 salary range, which was understood to be solidly middle class. There were 5.5 million families in this category in 1929, 17.9 million in 1953. They accounted for 35 percent of the nation's population; they carned 42 percent of its income. These were the candidates for suburbia, the cream of the American marker.

Unlike the classic definition of the middle class as a collection of small proprictors, this newly minted middle class was made up almost entirely of employees of corporations, and thcir income came almost entirely from salaries. Morcover, there was a dectine in the number of families in the lowest income class, from 15.6 million to 11.7 million. The nation's total income was rising twice as rapidly as the population, so chere was a lot more wealth per person. And the distriburion oths new wealth as very ir with verage working people as the chief beneficiaries. By the beginning of the Populuxe era, America was materially more democratic than it had been at any time in its history. [...]

Those just starting households and having families constitute the group most desirable to home builders and manufacturers because they must make many large expenditures quickly. During the late 1940s and early 1950s, those doing so at a 'normal' age were augmented by an enormous number of people who had delayed doing so because of the Depression and World War II. [...] The number of automobile registrations inhe United States more than doubled during the decade after World War II. [...]

It had long becn obvious that this great spending spree could not continue indefinitely, and by 1952 businessmen began to worry. Not only were Americans finally catching up with their consumption, but the new prime generation of consumers that was about to come on line was the smallest ever. [...] The one good thing about this smaller eneration was ha it had more mony to spend, and ir wanted to spend it. [For example} dion yetion the car [encouraged] the frequent replacement of stylish dress, the Big Three [motor manufacturers] cu      hyh periods when there were more potential buyers. (...]

Nearly uy ol the automobile companies, with more or less success. Home builders phased out the basic house and started coming up with new and elaborate models which could be fitted and personalized with a series of options and upgrades. Furniture makers and stylization, and certainly the artifacts that survive bear witness to the energy, creativity and sense of the ourrageous that they brought to their task. The arrival of new materials, particularly new kinds of floor and wall coverings, brought distinctly new looks. From both automobiles and clothing fashions came new colors and color combinations that rendered old rooms stodgy. [...]

Appliance manufacturers, some of which were owned by automobile companies, followed the same philosophy of bringing new looks and new many new products along the way. Appliances, which had taken on a streamlined form during preceding decades, became fatter and boxier, and they often sported decorative appliqués. They also were manufactured in color for the first time, something that introduced a new fashion element that was able to make kitchens and cheir appliances appear dated in a shorter cime. [..] The television set, a less practical and relatively new machine, went two-toned, modern. Early American, space age and every other trend that came along. Television, the most pervasive and iial v 1,nd mafacturers were aready startin to sell people on the idea of a second ser for the en for the arrival of color. Nor even the magic box could ecp the iplications of the demoac trough. [..]

Vacelig o chological dimensions of advercising, The Hidden Persuaders, came as a shock to many consumers when it appeared in 1957, although the methods discussed seem fairly blatant today. The book was strident in its condemnation of the way in which avertisng experts manipulate consumers, an it almost certainly overstated the power of advertising to change people's behavior. At the same time, it couldn't help bur evoke admiration for the cleverness of the industry, how much it had learned about people and the wit with which it used the information. People enjoy being fooled creatively, never more so than during the Populuxe era. Was convincing people that a detergent is mild by putting it in a blue box and that it is powerful by using an orange box any more fraudulent than a car that copies the look of an airplane or an apple pie made out of crackers? All were more often admired than condemned.

## DESIGN AND STYLING

Material democracy had been achieved nor through any arts-and-crafts uropia, or through a socialist forced redistribution of wealth, but rather through the workings of big business and its expertise in making people fcel good about its products. Progress was not merely an abstraction, but an array of goods that could be touched, switched on, photographed, improved and fantasized about. [... Some who cared about fine design and well-made products felt that democracy was on trial during this period and that it was failing miserably. They looked at the landscape of subdivision houses, the gaudy cars in the showrooms, the profusion of overweight furniture standing on tiny legs, and despaired that aristocracy had done things so much better. 'We have yet to prove that a democracy can produce a beautiful environment,' wrote the architect

Edward Durrell Stone in a 1959 article, "The Case against the Tailfin Age.' [..]

The standard was, of course, that of the marketplace. Products were judged not by how well they were made, or how well they did their jobs, buc by how well they sold. Indeed, the American tradition of industrial design is based on just this premise. In contrast wich the tradition of craftsmanship exemplified by William Morris and his followers, and with the ideology of technical expression thar arose wich the Bauhaus and European modernism, American industrial design has been concerned most of all wich inducing consumers to accept, understand and use a product. [.]

It is p proach in the changing image of the ideal kitchen. During the carly 1950s, when families were still cng up afer the war, a moden kien was simply an old-fashioned kitchen to which many new appliances had been added. Each of the appliances was slightly streamlined. Each stood on its own, a separate piece of machinery. [...] At the dawn of the Populuxe era [ .} appliances were no longer seen as objects in the kitchen. Rather, they were the kitchen. Modern life was close to inconceivable without them. Thus, appliances were increasingly built into kitchens [...] as part of an overall decorating scheme. Appliances no longer had to be dramatized; they were thoroughly domesticated. [..]

![](images/1c7ca743c08d7ee84e4ff6339b88acc0fb4e3950b6c68821625bd011fb22e547.jpg)  
Knopf, 1987, p. 67.

This new perception of objects as part of a way of life rather than as tools for carrying our a particular task required that design be thought about in a whole new way. The individual object became less important. Design for mass production involved more sociological insight than technical expertise or aesthetic intention. It is likely that even the famous Good Design shows a  Muse o Moden Art which wer tying to fight this trend, ended up being a part of it. They were intended to spotlight well-made, visually graceful objects for everyday use.Their effect was to let a small group of consumers signal that their households had the approval of the Museum of Modern Art. [...]

By traditional standards, this approach to making and using things was enormously wasteful. It did not mercly encourage but depended on the discarding of valuable materials, the scrapping of things that still worked. Throughour the first half of the 1950s, intellectuals condemned American behavior as wasteful [... but ...] by the latter half of the decade, the argument had swung in the other direction. The economic commentator Peter Drucker argued, for example, that the apparent wastefulness was actually a form of subsidy which allowed those at the lower end of the economic ladder to partake of the same pleasures as everyone else. Thus, a person who wanted a stylish and exciting new car would usually discard an old car that still ran well but was no longer in style. A poorer person could then buy it used for less than it was really worth. [..]

The celebration of the production and sales process at the expense of the products proved l    i Nelson, design director of Herman Miller furniture, advocates of modern furniture design. The postwar period was a kind of golden age of American design. Charles and Ray Eames produced a series f designs tha proved to e classics did Charles Eames's sometime collaborator Eero Saarinen. Harry Bertoia's wire chairs and Nelson's 'coconu'chair and storage walls were also familiar parts of the American landscape. Berween them. Knoll and Herman Miller seemed to be making possible the creation of a distinctly American modernism in furniture. Like Bauhaus and most other prewar European modernist designs, these pieces sought to reflect a machine-based sciy. But the European pieces were primarily formal experiments, handcrafted objects which sought to questin miliarage of particlar s. d they were concerned most of all wih stture. [..} Of the Europeans, only Alvar Aalto had eig p i er f te pocese  wc they would be madc. And Aalto worked only in laminated wood.

The postwar American designs, by contrast, followed the native method of close integration of design, marketing and production. And they were designed to take advantage of new materials and new technologies. Perhaps the definitive example was the foam and molded fiberglass 'womb' chair, designed by Eero Saarinen and introduced by Knoll in 1948. This bucket seat proved to be the protorype for a great many variations on the themc ofmolded sculptural seat standing on frail metal legs. 'Ass trays.' Frank Lloyd Wright called them. but they starred turning up everywhere. [.]

The designs of Eames, Saarinen, Nelson, Bertoia and others were generally informal. They sought to be light and comfortable. They were realistic about the body and did not make it conform to a particular sculptural form, but racher let the furniture mirror the shapes and motions of te body on the surfaces where contact would be made. This furniture was, therefore, a bit more ungainly than the canonical 1920s modernist forms. but it was closer to modernist ideals of practical, affordable, machine-made furniture. It as extely infuntal, nd iid turn people's lives, if not necessarily in their houses. Moreover, it conveyed a sense of the new and the dynamic.

But the work of the innovative American designers did not ultimately add up to the kind of environment in which people actually lived. This had something to do with the nature of the furniture industry iself, which was divided into many small companies, most of them making only chests or upholstered goods or metal furniture. The new furniture used combinations of materials and technologies that most manufacturers defined as being outside of their particular business. And while Knoll and Herman Miller did pursue the resiential market in their arly years, their stand ards of construction and the prices they had to charge made their products much more attractive to the corporate and institutional market, where the durability they offered was more in demand. And the immediate and widespread acceptance of modern American furniture for institutional uses probably worked against its use in the home. The Populuxe house was viewed as being an island of individuality, a refuge from the world a are. If a chair could be found at the school library,i probably would not be found in the home.

Nevertheless, a lot of modern and contemporary furniture was sold for the home. Much of Eames was doing, but it generally left out the technological innovation. The commercial-grade furniture, called"borax' and waterfallin he trade, was made essentially the same way whether it was labeled 'contemporary,' 'traditional' or 'Early American.' Today, one can look at the adverrising of a big company like Krochler and not be able to tell the traditional from the modern withour a label. [...] The word 'modern' was often used for furniture that was a bit more daring in design, a b more fashionable, than the furniture desnated as 'contemporary.' Confusingly, the terms were often used in a different way, wich 'modern' used as an antonym for 'formal.' describing an attitude toward living and room arrangement that could embrace traditional pieces as well as those that were consciously contemporary. But both definicions of 'modern' implied an aspiration to lightness, boch in color and in line. (...] Contemporary tables and small decorative objects such as ashtrays took on dynamic asymmetrical forms, such as boomerang or palerce shapes, while lrger contemporary objects tended to be boxy. There were no moldings, and chin, slightly splayed. round metal or meral-footed wooden legs were almost universal. [...]

The orher feature of contemporary design which was found in both high-style and mainstream homes was wall-mounted shelving, either attached to the wall or featuring thin metal stanchions. Several decorating books published at the time identified such shelving, along with what was termed 'the color revolution,' as the decorative touch by which the era would be known. Like e ole lmphis selvigapperd sericble and almost weighcless, with an industrial quality that was not particlarly vebearing The elve defined a grid on which all sorts of objects, from books to record players to clusters of African sculpture, family pictures or souvenir bric-a-brac, could be placed. The grid was an important motif, one that was identifed both with the walls of new downtown office buildings and wich the paintings of Mondrian, whose infuence had trickled down to cigarette advertisements and the decoration of kitchen cabinets.

Extracted from Hine, T. Populuxe, New York: Alfred A. Knopf, 187. Taking Of. pp.3-14 'The Luckiest Generation.' pp. 15-36, and 'Design an Stylingpp.59-81. Copyright © 1987.2007 by Thomas Hine, published by The Overlook Press, New York, NY. AlIl rights reserved. www. overlookpress.com.

# THE KHRUSHCHEV KITCHEN: DOMESTICATING THE SCIENTIFIC-TECHNOLOGICAL REVOLUTION

Susan E. Reid (2005)

In the cosmos, socialist science had proved its superiority with the launch of the first Sputnik in October 1957. The kitchen, meanwhile — and the conditions of women's work in general - remained the site of the Soviet system's humiliation and a symbol of its backwardness.' In the notorious confrontation between the superpowers at the American National Exhibition in Moscow 1959, ir was the state-of-the-art kitchen of the model American home that served Vice-President Richard Nixon as the ideal platform from which to challenge Soviet state socialism. For if US superiority in the space race and arms race was in doubt, capitalism's victory in the standard-ofliving race seemed assured.²

These were far from trivial matters to the Soof cialism was to be achieved through superior living standards rather than military might. It was an article of faith - and not only of Cold War polemics - that socialism would guarantee the best pos iins of people.' The Khrushchev regime repeatcdly indexed the imminent transition to communism to the achievement of superabundance and unprecedented prosperity, and devoted an extraordinary degree of attention to consumption and everyday. domestic life.

The promised abundance that would win the Cold War for socialism was to be attained by harnessing the achievements of modern science and rechnology. 'We stand on the threshold of a new scientific-technological and industrial revolution', declared Premier Nikolai Bulganin at the July 1956 Central Committee plenum.' The Scientific-Technological Revolution or 'STR' was a central term in official pronouncements of the Khrushchev era, which made it a defining characteristic of socialist modernity. In the postwar world, advanced science and technology were fundamental to the arms and space race as well as for international prestige. But only the socialist system, founded on scientific principles, it ws argud wa cpable of fullyplyi technological revolution to benefit human life. The Third Party Programme adopted in 1961 - the definitive ideological staterent of the Khrushchev period - identified social progress with scientific and technological progress. It was to be achieved through electrification of the whole country, comprehensive mechanization of production, and civilian applications of atomic energy and chemistry. [..]

In the Marxist tradition, the source of woman's backwardness was her confinement to the home and imprisonment by domestic labour. As Lenin wrote, woman would 'remain a domestic slave in spite of all liberating laws' as long as housework remained isolated labour conducted in the home. The solution, for Lenin and Aleksandra Kollontai - as for a series of socialists and feminists before chem, from the German Social Democrat August Bebel to the American feminist Charlorte Perkins Gilman - lay in the abolition of the individual en socialized housework and collective childcare. Only the development of services and new, collective forms of everyday living, would, according to Lenin, 'in practice be able to liberate woman, and reduce and eventually annihilate her inequality wich man.

In the late 1920s and carly 1930s, some experiments were made wich the restructuring of domestic life through the establishment of communal and kitchen-less dwellings. Ernst May designed housing of this sort for the new 'socialis city' Magnitogorsk, while Moisei Ginzburg and the Constructivist architectural group OSA (Union of Contemporary Architects) developed minimal kitchen niches for the Narkomfin communal house, Moscow, in 1930. However, the priorities of rapid industrialization in the early 1930s wcre in conflict with the investment required to transform cooking and childcare from isolated homecrafts into scientifcally-organized, socialist service industries. Moreover, beginning with the dissolution of the Zhenotdel (the women's section of the party) in 1930, the Stalinist state retreated from the commitment to the iy of the family and the emancipation of women. To promote social stability and increase the birth rate, new legislation in 1936 and 1944 reinstated the family as the pillar of society and asserted women's social obligation to reproduction as well as production." [...}

The retrenchment of the Stalin years and the image of the woman-centred home as a dream of normality left a legacy to the Khrushchev era chat was only partly addressed. [...] On the one hand, the project of the 1920s to build a 'new way of life' was resumed, including the aim to emancipate women from kitchen slavery. On the other, as will be shown, both the discourse of modern Soviet living and the acrual, built form of housing in the Khrushchev era, reconfirmed the individual family home as the site of reproductive labour, and the housewife as its isolated and unpaid workforce. [...}

Only by active participation in the sphere of production and public life could a person fully realiz hersel as the integrated, all-round individual who would be a citizen of communism. But, as (Khrushchev] recognized, the combined burden of job, childcare and housework prevented many women from fully engaging in social and political life." His preferred apprach to the dual task of raising living standards and liberating women lay (in accordance wich the declared 'return to Leninist norms') in the expansion of communal services and public institutions such as mass housing, schools, healthcare, and public dining facilities. These were promoted under the seven-year economic plan adopted in 1959." The need for service establishments would be fully met wichin years, promised the new Party Programme, and free public dining would be provided at workplaces by the 1970s." [.]

But the intensive housing campaign of the late 1950s was based not on the model of the communal houses and social condensers of the 1920s, intended radically to restructure everyday life, bur on small-scale apartments designed for a single nuclear family." Far from rendering the kitchen obsolete, they provided families with their own dedicated kitchen space, often for the first time in their lives. As an astute Western commentator Alexander Werth, noted in 1962, 'All this business about "communal feeding" and "boarding schools for children" seemed in contradiction with the present tendency to cultivate the family, to give individual fats to cvery family' ]

Women's labour in the home seemed parcicularly resistant to reform because it consisted of craditional practices passed down the generations from mother to daughter and unconsciously perpetuated. By the postwar period, however, the concinual social and demographic upheavals of the twentieth century - urbanization and industrialization, revolutions and war - had done much to loosen the hold of traditional patterns of domesticity." Khrushchev's intensive housing drive accelerated the process of urbanization so that some had to adapt to apartment living.

Furthermore, the explicit commitment to providing one-family fats 'even for newly-weds'meant that many, or e t time, st u house n t own insteadof slotting into an established househod u -aw alongside other women in a communal kitche. These developments created opportunities specialists and professionals to step in with advi and reshape this tradition-bound domain. 77 period saw a food of household advice addresse to the khoziaika (housewife), which attempted to inculcate modernized household management practice in a generation of women struggling under their 'double burden'. [..]

![](images/7319a3f98e323c27e77de68eeccf772463b0c16536f5980594b81586a47ca994.jpg)  
Svi i Biar Contempnrary History 40(2) (2005), pp. 289-316, p. 307.

In the ideal world of planners and advicegivers, the rational plan of the kitchen, designed to facilitate woman's labour, became a regulator of her movement within it and directed and disciplined her body. [...] Compactness was the key to efficiency. It was also hard to avoid in the 'economical' kitchen of the ncw, one-family apartments, measuring between 4.5 and 6 square merres. In the interest of both "labour saving' and 'space saving', free-standing equipment was to be replaced by compact blocks ofunits along the wall, with suspended cabinets above." Wall-mounted cupboards had the added advantages that they did not take up precious floor space, and that they saved the housewife from having to bend down c ihe  its.c dishes hung over the sink saved time in onc of the most wearing domestic casks - dishwashing." At the same time, the compact arrangement of equipment also saved on plumbing and wiring. Thu, labor saving spac saving and eny of connecting to utilities were identified. The small dimensions of the kitchen, imposed by requirements of economy and as a pledge to its future elimination, bece a virtue, the surc it efficiency. [..]

Compactness, it should be noted, was also the founding rincip ofatnal kitcens devel fr scus iny part  uro b svig ndpace avigndep what is widely regarded as the historical prototype of the modern fited kitchen, the small-scale 'Frankfurt Kitchen', designed in 1926 by Austrian communist architect Grete Schütte-Lihotzky for Ernst May's public housing programme in Frankfurt-am-Main. Schütte-Lihotzky conceived te kitchen s  quasi-industrial sie of food production, looking to the most up-to-date transportation of the day, the tiny kitchen galley on epress trains, for a model of modern efficiency.

'The amount of work thar is done in restaurantcar kitchens only gets done because the kitchen is so small', she observed. 'Time-wasting walks are impossible."" (...]

The roots of the effciency ideology at work in the Sovier discourse of the Khrushchev kitchen can be traced ultimatcly to Amcrican capitalism, whether directly or mediated through carlier and recent European assimilations, including Russian ones. Soviet design and advice litcrature of the lare 1950s-60s has clear echoes of Christine Frederick's 'scientific management' which applied to "household engincering' Taylorist management principles appropriated from industry. In the 1920s and 1930s Frederick's book Household Engineering: Scientific Management in the Home (1915) was translated into a number of European languages and interpreted through European household efficiency ideologues such as Paulette Bernège and Erna Meyer." It was highly infuential on the conception of "labour-saving' kitchens in Europe (including the Baltic States) as well as America in the 1920s. The ideology of labour efficiency and of industrial-style rationalization in the home also entered Russian and Sovier discourse and design practice at that time, as part of the campaign for the new way of life. It was one aspect of broader enthusiasm for Taylorism and Fordism, which was shared by Lenin and capitalists alike, seeming to transcend differences of political ideology."

The Khrushchev kitchen was founded, then, on common principles of modern industrial planning such as also underpinned the development of the American fitted kitchen, promoted at the Amcrican National Exhibition in 1959 as the embodiment of the American dream. This convergence belies assertions that ideological irrcconciability' with the capitalist camp would concinue in spite of peaceful co-existence and economic competition. Indeed, American models were closely studied by Soviet designers and planners. Nevertheless, the American dream kitchen was far from compact and had too many appliances to be affordable on a mass scale in Soviet condicions.

Moreover, it was ideologically inappropriate, designed not for the working woman, but to frame the middle-class 'professional bousewife'. It was her surrogate domain to compensate for her lack of a place in the public arena. Contradicting the officially-espoused commitment to equality and emancipation of women, this opened an angle from which to dismiss the unaffordable model as one with which the Soviet Union would not even try to 'catch up and overtake' As a construction engineer wrote in the comments book at the American exhibition in Moscow, referring to the fully-automated Whirlpool 'Miracle Kitchen' shown there: 'In the "miracle kitchen" a woman is just as free as a bird in a miracle cage. The "miracle kitchen" shown at the exhibition demonstrates America's last word in the fcld of perfecting obsolete forms of everyday living which stultify women."

While the American housewife was represented inthe trall o he many possesions, t most radical advocates of the scientific kitchen in the Soviet Union claimed its austere, standard form as a liberation. One such, G. Liubimova (in an article that makes a rare and telling reference to Schütte-Lihotzky as well as to the Russian Construccivists) ustfied proposals euip new flats with standard, fully-ftted kitchens in spite of the added cost to the stace, because 'a racionally-organized interior enables a certain "automation" of domestic processes . the person is not distracted and gets less exhausted. Furchermore, "in an apartment equipped according to scientifically worked-out norms, when using the objects a person does not fix attention on them, does not fetishize things, and this has an educational significance'." Standardization of urilitarian routines and domestic frcings would thus have the added advantage of combating the regressive influence of the nuclear household and of the increased availabilicy of consumer goods. Thi con all him difference in principle between the ideal Khrushchev kitchen and its better-known American counterpart. [...]

The story remains to be told of how Soviet people in practice arranged and used their kitchens, obeyed, negotiated or ignored the norms that were so insistently promulgated. Numerous obstacles lay in the path of even the most zealous convert. The Scientific-Technological Revolution failed to march triumphant into people's homes, not - or not necessarily - because people resisted it, but because of Sovict economic realities. [..] Fully-fitted kitchens were rarely installed in the new Aats built under Khrushchev; for reasons of economy and speed, only minimal cquipment was provided. The Khrushchev kitchen was not a factory-made unit in the way that the Frankfurt kicchen was, but required a large element of reciprocal effort from its occupants, depending on their readiness to follow the ubiquitous advice, and on their resourcefulness and skills to find, build and istall the equipment chemselves.Acknowledging the failings of the system, domestic advice practical nature: to help the occupant construct or adapt cabinets, and install the recommended devices themselves.31

T ped with electric devices and hygienic surfaces, should be understood as one of the key symbols of Khrushchevism and a demonstration of che will to extend the Scientific-Technological Revolution into the 'private'domain of domestic everyday lie. Its failure to materialize, however, marked the limits of the Soviet order's abilicy to impose its version of modern living on its citizens.

# ALL THAT GLITTERS IS NOT STAINLESS Reyner Banham (1966)

Two of the most important resources of modern design are about a hundred and twenty years old now; one is the plastics industry, the other is the tradicon o worry about the stateof theart. As far as plastics are concerned, celluloid molding dates from the work of my countryman, Alexander of American, Charles Goodyear, a decade carlier.

In the divided culture in which we live and work, we have to remind ourselves who founded plastics, but I don't have to remind you that design worry was founded by old John Ruskin and young William Morris, not to mention stylish Gottfried Semper in Germany and functional Horatio Greenough in the United States, in those same midle years of the last century. Yet, if you cast eyes on the visible scene or lay hands on the tangible environment, you will know that the eff of the plastic ndusty as been ast an all-pervasive.

But what has the tradition of worry about the stare of the art done for design that can be compared to the avalanche of new materials and products that the plastics industry has cmptied over the face of the earth?

One ching it has done is to worry about new materials like plastics, new materials that drive out old familiar ones because their performance can be more accurately specified chan any ancicnt craftsman could select wood. Such materials can be more accurately specified by che designer, but remain totally inscrutable to the ultimate consumer.

So, John Q. Public, the well-known car buff, looks at the badge on the front of the 1967 GT Supremo Saddlesticched Hodad Fastback and can't y lookig whether it is an exquisie specimen of the goldsmith's art sealed under crystal glass or just crafty vapor gilding on the back of one-shot styrene molding - chough he has his suspicions! Or another instance: The red minicar in which the Banham family used to thread its way deftly through the wild, swinging, miniskirted London scene was protected all around its lower perimeter by a strip of what was implied to be stainless steel but proved to be metal foil sealed under clear plastic. It glittered like stainless, but it wasn't. What is more, it did a berrer job than stainless could have done by not introducing cerrain risks of snagging, tearing and spearing that make stcel dangerous in an accident. Yet the classic tradition of design worry would insist on genuine stainless and denounce the plastic trim as a cheap substitute or a trick. To satisfy the conscience of design (a monster on which I shall have much more to say, we have to be impaled by genuine stainless steel.

Fortunately, design worry offers other precepts to guide us: For instance, the plastic trim would be OK if it didn't pretend to be something it isn't. Bur that would deprive us of some things we all clearly love: Glitter, high finish, and shine. These are visual qualities that plastics have democratized, so thar the average parking lot or appliance showroom bounces back the light in a way previous ages probably saw only on the serf-polished armor at aristocratic jousts and tourneys.

And this love of glitter is not just a vulgar dream of the silly and underprivileged. The greatest generation of design cheorists, who flourished from 1910 to 1930, all loved glitter and taught us to love it too. Frank Lloyd Wright rejoiced in the lights of Chicago by night; Marinetti saw the new age reflected in the light bouncing back from control-consoles and electrical plants; Gropius called for buildings like crystal symbols, and Mies van der Rohe built them; Fernand Leger was struck by the magic of light on metal on a field gun; Lc Corbusier and Marcel Brcucr pur that magic into production on furniture; Sir Herbert Read invited us to admire instruments and vessels of stainless steel.

Industrial design rides upon the back of an industrial complex that exiss primarily to satisfy such desires of man as universal glitter. But why does the heart of man desire it? Why did the great masters of modern design teach us to echo, in life, Goethe's dying demand, "Mchr Licht"? What is the source of this modern desire that plastics above all can supply?

Philosophers, semanticists, psychologists, historians (and there's a bundle of resources every progressive egffn school inceasingly c l hm hn hsT - notic effct  itter nd rihtness caught even Saint Thomas Aquinas, perhaps because shining" is a word that rings with virtue chroughour Holy Writ; the face of Moses shone, and the house of a latterday patriarch was called Taliesin Shining Brow. That old magic still works today fr ciers pushg tohaste and or ps pushing the millennium, asking the Almighty "look shining a new styles of architecture. change of heart!" And that stunning phrase from W. H. Auden sums up exactly what modern design has done for the ancient magic of glitter. The great surce of our preoccupation with the shininess of modern design is that it symbolizes an is  mi o mill in whi iuty nd i rts o de w born.

The generation of great gray eminences who presided over the birth of modern design as a responsible profession grew up in the grubby, care-exture word at-Vriny. They saw that industry's main product - soot - irremediably caking the unpolished and unwashable surfaces of its main structural materials, brick and cast iron, and they complained, in the words of a great visionary of light, Paul Scheerbart: "Backstein-Kultur tut uns nur Leid (brick brings us only hurt)." No wonder that Adolf Loos wanted the walls of the heavenly city to be Aush and smooth from top to bottom and that his gencration saw the rask of design in Augean terms; a total, global cleanup. And not just a physical cleanup. In the wricings of Le Corbusier, for instance, words like "health" and "morality" frequently turn up in adjacent phrases of the same sentence as twin attributes of modern design. One of the grear intellectual resources of our times, the concept of moral improvement through desig, is also onof ts most powcrful sources o intellectual confusion. I suppose it goes back to that lovable Victorian nut. A. W. N. Pugin, and his implied proposition that the revival of truly Chritian pned hicture would back the Age of Faith he supposed to have been in full swing when pointed, or Gothic, architecture appeared for the first time round.

Ever since, the design theorists and worriers over the state of the are have insisted that style betrays the moral intention of the designers: Arr Nouveau equals decadence; Expressionism equals selfishness; white walls and flat roof equal care for functional performance; redwood and roof overhangs equal care for human values; glass boxes equal inhuman disregard for people; chromium brightwork equals commercial swindle; and so forth.

Not one of these propositions is demonstrably true, yet cach has been (and many still are) passionately believed in or persists as unrevised prejudice. Yet we know thar many flat-roofed and white-walled modern buildings were indifferencly designed for functional performance.

Or again, when General Motors came up with the near, sweet, almost chrome-free body shell of the first Corvair, moralizing design critics congratulated Detroit on mending its wicked ways. Yet this is the model that Ralph Nader and all the litigants assert is a deathtrap sold by General Motors with full knowledge of its instability. It seems that the glitter of a morally sound style does not guarantee a stainless reputation to the product in use.

Yet the moral reassurance seems to be remarkably necessary in all branches of design, and professional designers go to camp meetings in the mountains to be told what's right and what's wrong. No other profession, not even those bound by massive oaths of probity like the Hippocratic oath of the medicals, has this rage to keep itself morally pure by public self-examination. A man sitting next to me at a parcicularly bitter session of an architectural conference in England some time ago said incredulously, "Do dentists have mectings like this?"

This moral preoccupation is onc of the principal driving forces of modern design and could be a great guarantee to the general public were it not so self-regarding. Thar line of W. H. Auden I quored earlier reminded me of something contemporary from Louis MacNeice:

Our freedom as free-lances

Advances towards its end;

The earth compels, upon it

Sonnets and Birds descend;

And soon, my friend,

We shall have no time for dances.

The concept of the freedom of free-lances coming to an end is sure to strike a familiar note, if only because it has been said so often at places like Aspen. Year after year, men have stood ar the microphone and preached hellfire, the population explosion, and the sands of time running out. We love it, gladly agree that our time for dancing has come to an end, and resolve to go out and do better by taking the situacion more seriously.

Better? What's so good about a world where the designers have salved their consciences by taking everything so seriously that poetry falls flat, the birds are all grounded, and nobody dances. One of humanity's main motives for surviving the bomb, the baby boom, and the final solidification ll orit, revive the watusi and the pavane, and clip on the optional equipment generally. Humanity is nor going to survive just so designers can work up a high polish on their consciences, nor will it thank them for being less aurarchic and more systematic in their thinking if the products don't get any better.

Any person in his right mind will know that conscience divorced from function helps nobody, yet the design profession at large is chronically pnnn. Why? The answer again lies in the source from which modern design has sprung: the concept of the designer as some kind of artist. On the one hand, this idea descends from the traditional view of the architect as one who imposes cultural values on the mere construction of buildings; on the other hand, from the William Morris fiction of the designer-craftsman as an arcist in the sense in which the nineteenth century understood the artist, that is, as a free spirit answerable only to himself. If the public didn't understand the artist's work, so much the worse for the public, especially as t sill hivi.

Morris himself took a more socially responsible view than this, but my reading of many of his professed followers is thar they believed the only good product was one that brought pleasure to its producer. You will hear this proposition usually in the guarded and inverted form that mass production is evil because it brings no pleasure to the worker, but which ever way you phrase it, the whole conception is ancisocial and perverse. No more in design than in dentistry can society accept that the first responsibility of its scrvants is pleaheselves. An  e  coss The public conscience of the design profession tells it that it cannot give absoluteallegiance t the promptings of its private conscience. The designers a social beig cnonts th designer as a creative individual in an unresolvable dilemma, and he is glad to have any hellfire demographer or revivalist cybernetician come and hand him a ready-made answer to this problem or any of the others.

For the conscience problem is no more than typical, in its inner contradictions, of the situation that modern design has inhcrited from its historical sources. Whereas most of its physical resources have unequivocal value - new materials, new production methods, etc. - many of its psychological sources bear signs of the confusions amisdirections ha have resulted fom tng ke   .

Take the concept of the basic design course, the Vorkurs, or what have you. There is a great primary source if you like! As a concept, it has a noble simplicity to it; the student is to be returned to zero and made to begin again with the clementary materials and primary relationships of his craft. Thc sophisticated shall be brought low, the onest nd umble shal li.

Yet all over the world the "Bauhaus system." as this kind of teaching is often called, is in disarray and contention. Most design educators seem not to know where clse to start, even whilc admitting that the system doesn't work. What has gone wrong? Firstly, and obviously, it never was a system, it was a body of teaching methods under constant revision from 1919 to 1933 by a body of remarkable men. And it was the men who mattered - the system never went wrong while it was administered by men who had been through the Bauhaus mill. It never went wrong on Joseph Albers, for instance.

But the other thing to note is that these old Bauhausler' kept the methods under revision. There is startlingly little resemblance berween what Moholy-Nagy was doing in his last years at the Institute of Design in Chicago and what he and Albers had been doing in Weimar in 1923. And I suspect the driving motivarion to change everything was that one of the chief justifications of the original Weimar course invented by Hannes Itten had disappeared it was no longer necessary to disabuse students of ingrained visual prejudices, and it is even less nccessary roday.

A lot of things have happened to people since theBauhaus was youngchings like junk sculp-, han-eld mois, Ba, acti paig, Hell's Angels, surrcalism, custom-car shows, Op art, Henry Moore, Cinerama, and so on. As a result, people have become sophisticated - remarkably so - and far less visually prejudiced. Beady little eyes that can tell stainless from spray chrome at fifty paces and prefer the latter because it is more jokey clearly need a very different type of education from what suited the mystical peasants who crawled out of the Biedermier woodwork to join Gropius at Weimar.

Something else that has happened to people since the Bauhaus is, of course, the Bauhaus - and industrial desin generally It has not gone unnoticed, cither; the public has picked up some fmu nm n ven   fiy u . A top New York design pundit told me how he observed suburban housewives admiring some Barcelona chairs in Macy's. His comment: "God, it was horrible!"

My own comment on that comment would be: W      n leh 'thank you' as you drown." But plenty of other design people would have responded in the same yAfer undd years  more of rrdn the bad taste of the public as one of design's major problems, it can be difficult to adjust to the idea that they may now be on your side and may have stopped throwing rocks.

F    e want to adjust. The beliefthar design is a thankless task definitely appeals to the martyr complex that design has inherited from the artistic forebears. An uhe  b    as guarantee to their consciences that they were in teigt  deig s  part o t reat gressive do-gooder complex of ideas based upon that the public must be led, cajoled, sticked, and garroted onward and upward.

This evil backside on the face of publicconcern is one of the nastier aspects of worrying about the state of the art. It leaves behind some unpleasant questions, such as: Is the shine on the brow of the designer as he hands out the tablets of his lore the ue stainless litter of Messianic inspiration,  s it just the spray chrome of self-righteousness? Too many of the great unquestioned assumptions on which modern design is based have begun to peel and fake of late; neither they nor their advocates appear to be quite such stainless representatives of the shining new world as once we thought. It is high time we checked to see which ones have rusted through and must be junked, which need to go back in the plating tank. and which only need a wipe over with the silvercloth.

# GUIDE TO FURTHER READING

On the development of British modernism prior to the Utility scheme, see Paul Greenhalgh's 1995 essay 'The English Compromise: Modern Design and National Consciousness, 1870-1940'. In addition to Urility, the British response to the Second World War was also notable in design terms for a particularly effective body of print propaganda; see Abram Games, Graphic Designer: Maximum Meani Minimum Means (200On exhibicing post-war British design,see Lucy Bullivant' Design or Better Livi ublicRne  Britaan Make I'ini ritiMake  ritish De Context 1946-86 (1986); also Design and Cultural Politics in Postwar Britain: the Britain Can Make It Exonf 194 (199.On tFestival  Britain, eeAriFory, 'Festival Politicin Toct the Nation: The Fesival of Britain 1951(1976) and on the Festival Partern Group, 'New Imagery in Leslie Jackson, The New Look: Desig in the Fifties, (1998). A characterization of the period is provided in Bevis Hillier, Austerity/Binge: the Decorative Arts of the Forties and Fifies (197.Case studies of Briih esi cltu cura Jackon e ACn e Policy 194560', (1991); Andrew Jackson, 'Labour as Leisure: The Mirror Dinghy and DIY Sailors'. (2006) and John Hewitt, "Good Design in the Market Place: The Rise of Habitat Man' (1987).

For more by thc Independent Group, see Banham's "A Throw-Away Aesthetic' and Richard Hamilo' article The Persuasive Image', both from 1960. Refective analyses include Anne Massey' 'The Independent Group as Design Theorists', (1985) and "The Independent Group: Towards a Rcdefinition' (1987) and her book The Independent Group: Modernism and Mass Culture, 1945-59 (1995). Also, Nigel Whitcley's "Pop, Consumerism, and the Design Shift'1985) and Toward  Throw-Away Culture: Consumerism, "Style Obsolescence" and Cultural Theory in the 1950s and 1960s' (1987) and Ben Highmore's complex analysis 'Richard Hamilton at the Ideal Home Exhibition of 1958: Gallery for a Collector of Brutalist and Taschist Art' (2007).

As a comparative example of the politicization of design in national context see the studies of Germany by Eli Rubin, Synthetic Socialism: Plastics and Dictatorship in the German Democratic Republic (20), and Paul Betts, The Authority of Everyday Objects: A Cultural History of West German Industrial Design (2004). By the same author, see The Politics of Post-Fascist Aesthetics: West and East German Design in the 1950s' (2003) and "Building Socialism at Home: The Case of East German Interiors' (2008).

B Wi . BijkeThe Social Constructin f BakelieTowar  Thcory Invention8 Pen Spark' 'Plastics and Pop Culureand Ezio Manzis 'Objects nd Their Ski'inThe Plastic e: From Modernity to Post-Modernity (1990). Jeffrey L. Meikle reviews sceptical opinions in 'Material Doubts and Plastic Fallout', from his book American Plastics: A Cultural History (1995), which is extracted in this Reader, as is Alison J. Clarke's Tupperware: The Promise of Plastic in 1950s America lso Tupperwar  socil context, e Anath DeVida'Contai ModerniyThe Socal Life of Tupperware in a Mexican Indigenous Village' (2008).

A contemporary account of the Khrushchev Kitchen is given in Harrison E. Salisbury, "Nixon and Khrushchev Argue in Public as US Exhibit Opens; Accuse Each Other of Threats' (1959). Wider reading on Cold War design includes David Crowley and Jane Pavitt's Cold War Modern (2008). Also helpful are Sarah A. Lichtman's 'Do-t-Yourself Security: Safery, Gender, and the Home Fallour Shelter in Cold War America' (2006); Eli Rubin's "The Form of Socialism without Ornament: Consumption, Ideology, and the Fal and Rise of Modernist Design in the German Democratic Republic"; and Milena receives further consideration in Raymond G. Stokes's 'Plastics and the New Society: the German Democratic Republic in the 1950s and 1960s, in Style and Socialism: Modernity and Material Culure in Post War Eastern Europe (2000).

## SECTION 5

Postmodernisms, 19672006

# INTRODUCTION

# Rebecca Houze

ges for civil ights, protests n both Europe and the United States against the war in Vietnam, and iependencemovents i e ormer European colonis  Asia, Afric nd Lat Americ, any rs r e ut dec  ulal n pologcl at  n dition of postmodernity' confusing and unstable, in which our sense of self had become fragmented. Fredric Jameson described the 1970s and 1980s as a simultancously seductive and alienating period of late capitalism in which global commerce enabled the Auid distribucion of an enormous range of manufactured objects. For Jean Baudrillard, these objects are smulacra, or multiple copies for which there is no original. But we can't view postmodernism simply as a break from modernism; rather, as its name implies, it is in some senses a continuation. In Allthat Is Solid Mels into Air: The Experience of Modernity (1982), Marshall Berman described the instability of the contemporary world as a feeling that was also expressed in the work of some artists and thinkers of the late nineteenth and early twentieth centuries.

The five texts in this section reflect ways in which designers, critics and historians, beginning in the late 1960s, started to reinterprer design aesthetically, socially and philosophically. Many, infuenced by French post-structuralist lierary theory, sought to reveal the political and economic forces behind den h e ain e pation  nser'     p ing an object's meaning in unforeseen ways. We begin with 'A Significance for A&P Parking Lots or Learning from Las Vegas', by architects Robert Venturi, Denise Scott Brown and Steven Izenour, first published in 1967. The Las Vegas strip of the 1960s was a vast decorated landscape oriented to pular culture of consumption.Disconnected from the buildings themselves, oversized sgns along the highway advertised hotels, gas stations and casinos with sculptural forms and neon lights that seemed both magical and banal. Was this the world of tomorrow chat had been promised at the 1939 World's Fair? For Venturi Scott Brown and Izenour, the vivid asphalt world of Las Vegas, so diffrent from the spare and colourless modernist utopias of the early twentieth century, was a spiritualized real of the everyday whose forms, they believed, should be celebrated. This attitude had been shared as well by Reyner Banham and the Independent Group, whose work in the previous decade oriented tw Bui  neexc a isal stiu  the la wenh centy  epivn sne v por as he describes our experience of new media - especially flm, television, and the emerging digital ecnologis — in his ssay The Ecstasy of Communication' The contradictory views expressed in these t essay,jyl bracng  fruit capialism n tee hand skepi pess to design historical readings of this period.

German design historian Gert Selle, in his essay "Therc is No Kitsch, There is Only Design!", which was written on the occasion of the 1983 exhibition 'Genial Design of the '80s' at the IDZ (lnternationales Design Zentrum) in Berlin, argues in the spirit of Vencuri, Scott Brown and Izenour chat, A democratization of design has occurred .. A product culture — opulent, hedonistic, valid for all, and open to interpretation by all -has prevailed .'Echoing the 'Populuxe'aesthetic described by Thomas Hin in  text extracted in sectin  of this Reader, Sell emphasizes the sensuous nature of his prouct culture and the psychological meanings that objects take on as they are adopted by consumers. His valorization of material goods was especially relevant in a politically divided Berin, where capitalism was upheld by some as the arbiter of freedom and individualiy, while demonized by others for fostering excess and greed, and where Baudrillard's postmodern critique of the "hyperreality' of mass culture was perhaps less resonant.

Whereas Selle embraced the anonymity of mass-produced goods, the Italian decorative household g anfcueAletp vent  po a ispon e owell-known designers, such as Philippe Starck and Michael Graves. Perer A. Lloyd and Dirk Snelders question the idea of the 'omnipotent designer in their lively and critical examination of Starck's own accunt of the origins of his Juicy Salif Lemon Squeezer' designed for Alessi in the late 1980s. In his 1 sy Deat tuRolan Bart u tha ugh ot  y or supposed intentions of a work's maker into one's interpretation of it. Michel Foucault challenged Barthe'stheory the following year in his 199 essay"Wha is an Author?'Building on the work ooh Barthes and Foucault, as well as Adrian Forry's infuential book Objects of Desire: Design and Sociery Since 1750 (1986), Lloyd and Snelders demythologize Starck's exclusive role and attribute further credit to the object's users in determining the meaning of the lemon squeezer.

Ellen Lupton and J. Abbort Miller examine the evolution of graphic design practices in the 1980s and 1990s under the infuence of deconstruction, a school of thought based on the ideas of French philosopher Jacques Derrida, who argued in his book Of Grammatology - first published in French expanding  e theors o lingust Ferdinand  Saussre tha emeaning convyed through language is culturally construcred, and that writing disrupts and activates meaning, inserand Victor Burgin to Katherine McCoy and Daniel Libeskind, ofien aided by che possibilicies of digital technology, explored new ways of revealing the arbitrariness and disconcinuity of language and conceptual frames of reference.

Postmodernism is a complex and heterogeneous cultural tendency rather than a period style and it eerged  different imes i different eographical locations an withdiverseeiIndee very nature of postmodernist thought argued against homogeneity, unity or the completeness of the rand narrative' imposing instead a constant questioning, criticism and self-consciousness. While French literary critics such as Derrida, Barthes, Foucault and Baudrillard questioned the authoriy of myriad institutions from Western art and literature to prison systems, others, from Edward Said and Frantz Fanor to Gayatri Chakravorty Spivak and Homi K. Bhabha, applied this critical framework to problematize cultural identiy in the postcolonial words of Indi, Africa and the Middle East. Fashion. a medium that has long traversed national boundaries in its globalized production and consumption, magnatin JmaicDanchal Culue ublisheFashionheo i 0B Bk   wo anchall culture,drawing from an array of scholarship on subculture, feminist theory andracial polis, and shows how sarorial expression can obilizecncets o irony, history, parod nd ret Li pn uc n clashing an contrdictory eementsippe jeans, bondage tops and sparkling gold jewelyrevalg a bwdy and baroque love of excess and artificiality that references carnivalesque forms and traditions ofAfrican and Caribbean folk culture while als resisting hegemonic narratives of class, sex and race.

# A SIGNIFICANCE FOR A&P PARKING LOTS,OR LEARNING FROM LAS VEGAS

# Robert Venturi, Denise Scott Brown and Steven Izenour (1972)

ibstance for a writer consists not merely of those alities he thinks he discovers; it consists even ore of those realities which have been made ailable to him by the literature and idioms of his vn day and by the images that still have vitality the literature of te past.Sylistically, a we n express his feclings about this substance cither imitation, if it sits well with him, or by parody, it doesn't.'

Lei being revolutionary for an architect. Not the wious way, which is to tear down Paris and gin again, as Lc Corbusier suggested in the 20s, but another mote tolerant way; that is, to estion how we look at things.

The commercial strip, the Las Vegas Strip in rticular - the example par excellence - chaliges the architect to take a positive, non-chip--the-shoulder view. Architects are out of the bit of looking nonjudgmentally at the environent, because orthodox Modern architecture is ogressive, if nor revolutionary, utopian, and ristic; it is dissatisfed with existing conditions. odern architecture has been anything but perissie: Architects have preferred to change the isting environment rather than enhance what there.

But to gain insight from the commonplace nothing new: Fine art often follows folk art. manic architects of the eighteenth century discovered an existing and conventional rusic architecture. Early Modern architects appropriated an existing and conventional industrial vocabulary without much adaptation. Le Corbusier loved grain elevators and steamships; the Bauhaus looked like a factory; Mies refined the details of American steel factories for concrete buildings. Modern architects work through analogy. symbol, and image — although they have gone to lengths to disclaim almost all determinants of their forms except structural necessity and the program - and they derive insights, analogies, and stimulation from unexpected images. There is a perversicy in the learning process: We look backward a history and tradition to go forward; we can also look downward to go upward. And withholding judgment may be used as a tool to make later judgment more sensitive. This is a way of learning from eveyhing.

[....]

## VAST SPACES IN THE HISTORICAL TRADITION AND AT THE A&P

The A&P parking lot is a current phase in the evolution of vast space since Versailles. The space that divides high-speed highway and low, sparse buildings produces no enclosure and little on g z e high encosing frs. To move throgh landscape is to move over vast expansive texture: the megatexture of the commercial landscape. The The patterns of parking lines give direction much paving patterns, urbs brders and . But it is the highway signs, through their sculptural forms or pictorial silhouettes, their parcicular positions in space, their inflected shapes, and their graphic meanings, that identify and unify the megatexture. They make verbal and symbolic connections through space, communicating a complexity of meanings through hundreds of associations in few sections from far away. Symbol dominates space. Architecture is not enough. Because the spatial relationships are made by symbols becomes symbol in space rather than form in and the little building is the rule of Route 66.

![](images/c5abb8b1003ba5291669c91175489a6b70020f349ed8dc3beba494b4602c3900.jpg)  
o Massachusetts Instilute of Technology, by permission of The MIT Press.

The sign is more important than the architecture. This is refected in the proprietor's budget. The sign at the front is a vulgar extravaganza, the building   bck amot ey. Te S is the sign: The duck store in the shape of a duck called "The Long Island Ducklings," sculptural symbol and architectural shelter. Contradiction berween outside and inside was common in acture before the Moden Movement, particularly in urban and monumental architecture. Baroque domes were symbols as well as spatial constructions, and they are bigger in scale and higher outside than inside in order to dominate their urban setting and communicate their symbolic message. The false fronts of Western stores id the same thing: They were bigger and taller than the interiors they fronted to communicate the store's importance and to enhance the quality and unity of the street. But false fronts are of the order and scale of Main Street. From the desert on the highway the Wet o today, we can learn new and vivid lessons abour an impure architecture of communication. The little low buildings, gray-brown like the deserr, separate and recede from the street that is now the highway, their false fronts disengaged and turned perpendicular to the highway as big high signs. I y tkee  y te   pa desert town is intensified communication along the highway.

## LAS VEGAS SIGNS

Signs inflect toward the highway even more than buildings. The big sign - independent of the building and more or less sculpural or pictoli  potn eic and at the edge of the highway, by its scale, and sometimes by its shape. The sign of the Aladdin Hotel and Casino seems to bow toward the highway through the inflection in its shape. It also is three dimensional, and parts of it revolve. The sign at the Dunes Hotel is more chaste: it is only two dimensional, and its back echoes its front, but it is an erection 22 stories high that pulsates at night. The sign for The Mint Hotel on Route 91 at Fremont Street inflects toward the Casino several blocks away. Signs in Las Vegas use mixed media - words, pictures, and sculpture - to persuade and inform. A sign is, contradictorily, for day and night. The same sign works as polychrome sculpture in the sun and as black silhouette against the sun; at night it is a source of light. It revolves by day and becomes a play a  in al r istance. Las Vegas has the longe in the world, the Thunderbird, and the highest, the Dunes. Some signs are hardly distinguishable at a distance from the occasional high-rise hotels along the Strip. The sign of the Pioncer Club on Fremont Street talks. Its cowboy, 60 feet high, says "Howdy Pardner" every 30 seconds. The big sig a he Aladin Hotel as saw a litt with similar proportions to make the entrance to the parking. "But such signs!" says Tom Wolfe. Thsa  hape befoe whiche exisg vocabulary of art history is helpless. I can only attempt to supply name - Boomerang Modern, Paletter Curvilinear, Flash Gordon Ming-Alert Spiral, McDonald's Hamburger Parabola, Mint Casino Elliptical, Miami Beach Kidney." Buildings are also signs. At night on Fremont Street, whole buildings are illuminated but not through refection from spotlights; they are made into sources of light by closely spaced neon tubes. Amid the diversity, the familiar Shell and Gulf signs stand out like friendly beacons in a foreign land. But in Las Vegas they reach three times higher into the air than at your local service station to meer the competition of the casinos.

# THE ECSTASY OF COMMUNICATION Jean Baudrillard (1987)

[..] Advertising in its new version - which is no longer a more or less baroquc, utopian or ecstatic scenario of objects and consumption, but the effect of an omnipresent visibility of enterprises, brands, social interlocuters and the social virtues of cormunication - advertising in its new dimension invades everything, as public space (the treer, monument, marker, scenedisappears. It realizes, or, if one prefers, it materializes in all its obscenity; it monopolizes public life in its exhibition. No longer limited to its traditional language, advertising organizes the architecture ealnec  Be and che Forum des Halles, and of future projects (e.g., Parc dc la Villtte) which are monuments or anti-monuments) to advertising, not because :hey will be geared to consumption but because :hey are immediately proposed as an anticipated demonstration of the operation of culture, comnodities, mass movement and social fux. It is our only architecture today: great screens on which are reflected atoms, particles, molecules in notion. Not a public scene or tue public sace ut giganti sae of circulation, ventilation an :phemeral connections.

his loss of public space occurs contemporane-wi eos  iv cTe onger a spectacle, the other no longer a secret. Their distinctive opposition, the clear difference ext n n inter exacty ee the domestic scene of objects, wich its rules of l nd limits, and thsovreignty o  smolc space which was also that of the subject. Now this opposition is effaced in a sort of obscenity where the most intimate processes of our life become te irtual feedg ground o themed Loud family in the United States, the innumerable slices of peasant or patriarchal fe n Frenc television). Inversely, the entire universe comes to unfold arbitrarily on your domestic screen (all the useless information that comes to you from the entire word, like a microscopic pornography of the universe, useless, excessive, just like the sexual close-up in a porno film): all this explodes the scene formerly preserved by the minimal separation of public and private, the scene that was playcd our in a restricred space, according to a secret ritual known only by the actors.

C to the extent that it separated you from orhers - or from the world, where it was invested as a protective enclosure, an imaginary protector, a defense system. But it also reaped the symbolic benefits of alienation, which is that the Ocher exists, and that otherness can fool you for the becter or the worsc. Thus consumer society lived also under the sign of alienation, as a sociery of the spectacle.' But just so: as long as there is alienation, there is spectacle, action, scene. It is not obscenity - the spectacle is never obscene. Obscenity begins precisely when there is no more spectacle, no more scene, when all becomes transparence and immediate visibility, when everything is exposed to the harsh and inexorable light of information and communication.

We are no longer a part of the drama of alienation; we live in the ecstasy of communication. And this ecstasy is obscene. [...]

In yill hv of things, this forced extroversion of all interiority, this forced injection of all exteriority that the categorical imperative of communication literally signifies. There also, one can perhaps make use of the old meraphors of pathology. If hysteria a ptology exacebat ng of the subject, a pathology of expression, of the body's thearrical and operatic conversion; and if paranoia was the pathology of organization, of the structuration of a rigid and jealous world; then with communication and information, with the immanent promiscuity of llthese nerworks, with their continual connections, we are now in a new form of schizophrenia. No more hysteria, no m projeciv parna, proy speaking u tis sa eo poper t e hizopenc to great a proximity of everything, the unclean promiscuity of everything which touches, invests and penetrates without resistance, with no halo of private protection, not even his own body, t protect him anymore.

The schizo is bercft of every scene, open to everyhing in spite of himself, living in the greatest confusion. He is himself obscene, the obscene prey of che world's obscenity. What characterizes h is s e los  t  ta estrangement from the real, the pathos of distance and radical separation, as is commonly said: but, vey much t the contrary, the absolue proxmiy, defense, no retreat. It is the end of interiority and in intimacy, the overexposure and transparence of the world which traverses him without obstacle. He can no tonger produce the limits of his own being, can no longer play nor stage himself, can no longer produce himself as mirror. He is now only a pure screen, a switching center for all the networks of influence.
